aıchı [(c 0) (d d) hó I] @Machy 2022-02-05 #u0qfp0BlO $en +3
▯ alieves that ▯ is the case.
  Machy (2022-02-05): an alief is an idea that, while not considered to be true, nonetheless garners a strong emotional reaction. Like jumping at shadows after watching a horror movie: you believe there's nothing to be scared of, but you're scared anyway. The notion that there's a monster in those shadows is an example of an alief.
  Machy (2022-02-05): Best thing about conlanging is getting to make words for incredibly obscure concepts before filling obvious gaps like "fishing".
  lassc (2022-12-04): +1 for interesting concept and nice word form

aıja @robintown 2023-11-09 #wq3qEJ2TE $en +1
▯ gets ▯ to satisfy property ▯.
  robintown (2023-11-09): Etymology: Originally **ja**. **taı** was later added to avoid polysemy, which was further reduced to **aı**.
  robintown (2023-11-09): In colloquial speech, this word still commonly takes the form of 'ja' in serial verbs, but only in the falling tone.
  robintown (2023-11-25): Compared to **tua**, this word has the additional semantic nuance of enticing or helping another agent to enter some state.

aımu [(0) (d) hóq P] @Hoemai 2022-08-06 #zJJysQXen $en +2 %basic
▯ is not* the case.
  Hoemai (2022-08-06): This predicate expresses that its argument is false and that this disagrees with a proposition from the immediately preceding discourse (there should be one most salient proposition). Like the opposite of German "doch" and French "si", but as a verb. If no argument is provided, it defaults to the negation of the most salient proposition.
  Hoemai (2022-08-06): A: **Jảq chỏ hó jí** B: **Aımu chô hó súq**. A: "He really likes me!" B: "No, he doesn't."
  Hoemai (2022-08-06): A: **Gỉ hoı fảfuaq** B: **Aımu**. A: "That movie is good." B: "No, (it isn't)."
  robintown (2022-10-25): See also **jeha**
  Ilmen (2025-10-31): officialized: yes

aımu @official 2026-02-18 #YjRyKivSG5 $en ± %basic
polarity: ‘NEG.CONTR’; contrastive negation: not in fact (contradicting a salient positive claim)

aımuru @uakci 2022-11-19 #lZ77iFT1j $en ±
connective: indeed not X, and Y
  uakci (2022-11-19): similar to **buru** except that it "adds in" a **bu** if one isn't already present in the left connectand
  uakci (2022-11-19): so, both **bủ nủı aımuru sảo** and **nủı aımuru sảo** are meant to be correct and two different ways of phrasing the same thing (the former requiring less forethought), whereas **bủ nủı buru sảo** would mean the wrong thing (due to double negation)
  uakci (2022-11-19): < **aımu** + **ru**, in analogy with **buru** < **bu** + **ru**

ame @lassc 2025-03-18 #j6guO__qF $en +1
interjection: early termination of sentence
  lassc (2025-03-18): clarification: this is used as a way of essentially going "no, i shant" as you're saying something. i can imagine this being used in a variety of ways, like a playful way of getting the other person to "fill the blank" for what you were about to say, genuinely just stopping yourself before you say something embarassing, etc etc
  lassc (2025-03-18): cf. **sıanha**, which is very similar but avoids the connotation of discouragement of seeking further information and the connotation of negation of what was previously said
  lassc (2025-03-18): ex. «Tú ë tıjuı úmo chéq nä jaq jaı jí... Ma chı súq ꝡá... ame, ame, hahaha!»

áqna @robintown 2025-08-21 #cY-zD26_p $en +2
pronoun: ‘REFL.ND’; non-distributive reflexive pronoun
  robintown (2025-08-21): Subtly different in meaning to **áq**: "He kıaı hú poq áq" means "Those people (each) care for their own self (singular)", while "He kıaı hú poq áqna" means "Those people (taken as a group) care for themselves (the whole group)".
  robintown (2025-08-21): This word exists to fill out Kuna's theory of reflexives and subject-sharing, mostly. I don't quite know if this is aligned with how Hoemai imagines **áq** to work.
  robintown (2025-08-21): (But I do think it fits pretty snugly into the grammar)

áꝡa [  particle ] @uakci 2023-11-05 #G7fiMCu2v $en +3
pronoun: referring to the 1st, 2nd, or 3rd “person” when the individual in question does not want to have **jí**/**súq**/**nháo** used to refer to them
  uakci (2023-11-05): sometimes seen as ‘this one’ in english, or as consistent use of a 3rd “person” pronoun or a name instead of the «appropriate» personal pronoun
  uakci (2023-11-05): in a conversation between an **áꝡa** user and a non-**áꝡa** user, the former would use **áꝡa** to refer to themselves and **súq** to the listener, but the latter would use **áꝡa** for the listener. when both entities involved are **áꝡa** users then the referent is ambiguous but let me assure you they’re gonna enjoy it that way too

ba- @fagri 2023-01-06 #d7CKAbw2C $en +1
Prefix: lexicalizing modifier. Creates a new multi-word opaque vocabulary item out of the word this prefix attaches to and the word before it, with the semantic suggestion that the relationship between the two parts is similar to that invoked by the «be-» prefix
  fagri (2023-01-06): Example: The term «budo ba-hıao» ("noodle of harm") could be defined as an informal synonym for "snake"
  fagri (2023-01-06): Another example: the terms "malum prohibitum" and "malum in se" could be calqued into Toaq as «huı ba-juao» and «huı ba-huı»
  fagri (2023-01-06): The relationship can also be the one invoked by the «ce-» prefix
  fagri (2023-01-06): «Laı ba-teoq» "Question mark"
  fagri (2023-01-22): Can also be used for multi-part names
  uakci (2023-05-22): also see my redefinition #IHbG6HTs_

boq @el 2022-07-10 #FFehipBoz $tok ± %causality

boq @el 2023-01-06 #9NrxNpPfT $en ± %causality
▯ (evt) happening prevents ▯ (prop) from being the case
  el (2023-01-06): event-enabled version of the official defn to allow for use as a delta adverbial
  el (2023-01-06): kw: stops, precludes
  Ilmen (2025-10-30): I don't think ⟪boq⟫ is different from ⟪ca⟫, which IMO should also take abstract arguments: when you say ⟪eating this causes illness⟫, ⟪illness⟫ is abstract, you are not compelled to pick an ultraspecific individual result.

shê ꝡä faq [já] nä sahu [já]

boq @jiqci 2023-07-31 #xirISq-4dx $pl ± %causality
To, że ▯ zachodzi, zapobiega/uniemożliwia zajście ▯.

boq @Isi 2024-09-08 #oPKFOa9-4 $en ± %causality
predicate: ‘prevent’; ▯ prevents ▯ from happening.
  Isi (2024-09-08): The first slot is a thing and the second is an event.
  Isi (2024-09-08): The idea behind these slots being this way: (1) I feel that there's not a big difference between a proposition or event preventing something from happening vs. a thing preventing it from happening. (2) It's possible to prevent a single event from happening but not preventing a different event from making the proposition true; e.g. you could prevent someone's death (by curing their disease), but then they die anyways (for unrelated reasons).
  Isi (2024-09-08): Though I'm not sure if the semantics make sense when you refer to an event that doesn't actually happen.

⚠ cnopıt @jiqci 2025-01-13 #HqMAk53Uj $en ±
interjection: xnopyt

co- @Ilmen 2023-05-23 #g6y1a3Xzs $en ±
prefix: to display, noticeably do X with the hope that it is perceived by…

co @uakci 2022-08-21 #JFj7Z2C7b $en ±
cataphoric predicate: ▯ is something that satisfies a floating relative clause headed by **nheı**.
  uakci (2022-08-21): à la **nu** except that a **nheı** clause that follows specifies the semantic content of the **co**
  uakci (2022-08-21): **fả sa cỏ kủne jí, nheı rỉq hó da** ‘such a dog came up to me that it was green’
  uakci (2022-08-21): there are no restrictions on how to use **co** tone-wise; **có**, **cô**, even **cõ** seem all like fine options (maybe not **cò** though lol)
  uakci (2022-08-21): creative inversion example: **hẻ cỏ baq nhỏykuq nheı bủ kỉu chẻaqjıe hó** ‘such are liars that they can’t possibly be trusted’
  uakci (2022-08-21): **cõ bı gỉaqduy nháo nheı cả hóa kîeq jí** ‘they dance such that it [= their dancing] causes me to be disgusted’ (≈ **gỉaqduy nháo lũ cả hóa kîeq jí**)
  uakci (2022-08-21): **tỉ có baq sỏaq nheı jẻı sôı há chéq** ‘it was in a park, one’s fighting with each other’
  uakci (2022-08-21): thinking of allowing **nheı** to associate with DPs/CPs too (imagine removing the **jẻı** from this last example)

có [  particle ] @uakci 2023-05-30 #Rs8NuzD9J $en +2
pronoun: cataphoric; a placeholder that anticipates an extra noun phrase after the clause
  fagri (2023-05-30): Example usage: «Ca có wä jaı jı́, wä gaı jı́ sá shuao de gu»
  uakci (2023-05-30): akin to **nä** (and as such termable as a cleft) except the extracted noun phrase appears after the clause, not before. we assume that this NP appears after all adverbials (i.e., a full clause, right below CP) and participates in a cleft structure that’s exactly like **nä** but not pronounced (we’re able to pull this off because we don’t need to disambiguate the end of a noun phrase from the start of a predicate)
  uakci (2023-05-30): in other words, if a **nä**-cleft looks like [vP (fronted subject) [v' [v nä] [CPrel with hóa]], then a **có**-cleft shall look like [vP [v' [CPrel with có] [v ∅]] (fronted subject)]

có @robintown 2024-09-09 #sCGWzZtkJ $en ±
pronoun: ‘it.SUB’; correlative pronoun for subordinate clauses
  robintown (2024-09-09): https://toaq.me/Clause_Reform

chatua @Machy 2021-10-01 #M1gUCqqzk $en -1
▯ is a word that, while not actually a grammar word, has the function of helping other words rather than providing content of its own.

  Machy (2021-10-09): "coordinator word"
  Machy (2021-10-09): TAM words and semantic roles are also chatua
  Machy (2021-10-09): "empty predicate" - xorxes

chusıbo @uakci 2023-11-03 #4QoriqO6n $en ±
▯ is the average of ▯ (plural).
  uakci (2023-11-03): the idea is that this could extend to propositions: **chúsıbo, ꝡâ toı nháo súq** ‘their average treatment of you’, not really referring to any one event all in all but more to an abstract embodiment (cf. **sıbo**) of an average that could then be quantified with **ne** or talked about generically in other ways
  uakci (2023-11-03): in other words, these two sentences are meant to be congruent: **ꝡé he cha hôa báq, ë toı nháo hó** ‘that which, as a rule, is the manner of their treatment of you’ and **ꝡé cha hôa chúsıbo, ꝡâ toı nháo súq** ‘the way they treat you on average’

de- [  particle ] @Laqme 2024-09-19 #gAPROnUS8 $en +2
prefix for "adverbial auxiliaries" (verb → verb-ily do...)
  Laqme (2024-09-19): For example, **Dẹjaı jí, lä …** means "I happily …", and **dẹjaı marao** means "happily dance".
  Laqme (2024-09-19): This is useful for sneaking adverbs into serials: **shao dẹjuaı leo noaq** means "want to earnestly try to read"
  Laqme (2024-09-19): Only works on intransitive verbs, but you can first turn transitive verbs intransitive with other prefixes: **shao denịbıe sea jí** = "I want to hereafter rest"

déom @seaqtaq 2024-08-06 #lacrX6OJx $en ±
focus prefix: possibly X/perhaps X
  seaqtaq (2024-08-06): This is like daı as a focus prefix. Thus it has more permissive syntax. In simple cases, there appears to be no benefit, e.g. "Daı chuq náq zéo" ≈ "Chuq déom náq sá zeo". But in more complex ones, e.g. "Chuq líq rú déom náq", I believe the benefit is apparent in the rephrasing required with daı
  seaqtaq (2024-08-06): Haola déom X Y == Daı haola kú X Y
  seaqtaq (2024-08-06): Ergo "Chuq líq rú déom náq sá zéo" == "Chuq líq sá zeo rú daı chuq kú náq sá zeo"

  robintown (2023-01-15): I find the property version of **deoq** difficult to use in practice, and **duatua** to be a misnomer, so I propose this

But on the other hand, removing it with ⟪deoq⟫ would result in pretty much the same thing as either ⟪kuaq⟫ or ⟪ruaq⟫ I guess, unless we adopt my idea of ⟪▯ performs speech act ▯⟫, which is unspecific with respect to the nature of the speech act…
  Dembim (2026-02-13): Given <kuaq>/<ruaq>, I find <@Ilmen deoq> the superior definition. There is now also <nheam> for "▯ conveys the information necessary to conclude that ▯".

deoqzaosu @seaqtaq 2023-03-08 #rt2AkRPuN $en ±
▯ is one's communicative identifier/signature; ▯ is one's "voice" (not necessarily vocal)
  seaqtaq (2023-03-08): This is an abstraction of english's "voice" across every possible communicative medium, be it sound waves, the electromagnetic spectrum, tactile, olfactory, etc. Intended to be less anthropocentric, as the existence of this word means that it is not necessary to use the metaphor of "voice" to attempt to refer to communicative signatures in general. In addition to being less anthropocentric, it can be used for humans that can't speak as well. The word could be used to refer to the visual pecularities and idiosyncracies of the way one signs a particular sign language as well

du- @uakci 2024-02-12 #M790m0waZ $en ±
prefix: marks the following material as putative – not asserted as true and not part of the point one is making
  uakci (2024-02-12): like **du** but not quite. firstly, this can attach to any material – i imagine **dụ́sa** could be licit. secondly, while **du X** makes a *positive* claim about something seeming to be X,**dụ-X** makes no positive or negative claim – it strips assertive force from X

duaı [(c 1i) (d d) tá F] @official 2026-02-16 #_vnFnh5v4qf $en ± %modality
predicate: ‘must’; ▯ must satisfy property ▯.

duaı @robintown 2021-08-01 #BqjCzzUst $en ± %modality
▯ must be the case; ▯ must be the case if ▯ is the case.
  robintown (2021-08-01): This is the more regular form for modals.
  Laqme (2021-10-23): this expresses an obligation
  Laqme (2021-12-02): “deontic obligation”
  Laqme (2023-07-18): required
  Ilmen (2025-10-12): Why not a different word than the official ⟪duaı⟫? I interpret the original as having a focus on the person who is *reponsible* for the duty/obligation, and this new meaning doesn't allow expressing responsability.
  Ilmen (2025-10-12): Admittedly, the wording of the original definition is ambiguous and can also be interpreted as allowing ⟪the door ⟦duaı⟧/must be opened⟫ which is not about responsability. But I suspect this isn't what the original meaning is about, at least if we take into account the verb's frame.
  Ilmen (2025-10-12): I guess if this new definition were to be adopted, ⟪duaıgıla⟫ could work as a replacement for the previous meaning, but it's a bit longish…
  Dembim (2025-12-17): just fyi "A is obligated to bring about X" and "it is obligatory that A bring about X" are often considered interchangeable, at least in most cases. "Meinong-Chisholm reduction" I recall there being problems involving plural agents, but I'm not that familiar.
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Having looked into it a bit, I think the problems with impersonal obligation are rather serious. On the other hand, paraphrasing impersonal obligation in terms of an agent-indexed obligation operator alongside some gnomic/generic device seems likely (though I want to do more reading). So, I think <@official duaı> is the better definition, at least if we reconsider the class as ho rather than ta.

duaı @nano 2023-05-24 #YgT090Ue7 $tok ± %modality

duaı @jiqci 2023-07-31 #5djXkdtAI $pl ± %modality
▯ musi spełnić własność ▯.

duaı [(c 1i) (d d) hó I] @Dembim 2026-02-04 #EZwOO1vub $en ± %modality
predicate: ‘must’; ▯ (agent) is obligated to satisfy ▯.
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Pronominal_class: ho
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Frame: c 1i
  Dembim (2026-02-04): kw: deontic obligation
  Dembim (2026-02-04): see also: <taoduaı>
  Ilmen (2026-02-04): discriminator: 1
  Dembim (2026-02-12): Subject: individual
  Dembim (2026-02-12): Despite the appearance of "(agent)" in the definition body, the subject doesn't take the _role_ of agent in _this_ predicate but is rather a "moral agent" in the persistent sense.
  Dembim (2026-02-12): Distribution: d d
  Dembim (2026-02-12): Note that this differs from official in subject and pronominal class.

eq- @evie 2024-06-25 #NdFtFx4Pv $en +1

eq [(c c) (n n) tá I] @robintown 2023-01-18 #RaqwykTBg $en +3
▯ is/are ▯.
  robintown (2023-01-18): A generic copula. Often used with kinds, as in "Eq ní báq kato, ꝡë lụıkuaq shao sía poq, ꝡä bo hó hóa", but can also be used without kinds, as in "Bu eq jí póq, ꝡë joaı súna hóa nha". Aside from not being an object incorporating verb, eq differs from **jeı** only in how it interacts with kinds.
  kazoo (2025-01-12): example of how this differs from *jeı*: if you say "a pencil is green" with jeı, it'd imply "green is a pencil" is also correct. With eq, you're taking the safe option and you avoid this problem.

etu [  particle ] @uakci 2022-07-10 #J_cnp9RZG $en +2
determiner: **tu** but bubbles up to illocution-phrase level
  uakci (2022-07-10): **shı** equivalent would be **etushı**. based on **eru**; this determiner could be understood as "boiling down" to a (however long) coordination phrase involving **eru**
  uakci (2022-07-10): **hỉa etushı mẻa súq moq** 'how are each of you doing?'
  uakci (2022-07-10): spitballing-wise we could have **ésuq** for **etushı mẻa súq** and maybe this particle would never really get much use beyond that. who knows. no commitment intended!

étu @Laqme 2022-12-24 #ltpsbGJOp $en ±
determiner: "each X" (making a new speech act for each X)

foa @uakci 2023-01-03 #XJQsv4_Lf $en +1 %perception
▯ feels / is doing/faring in a way exemplified with property ▯; ▯ satisfies property ▯, and this is reflective of their mood/disposition/state of mind/status in life/how they’re doing; ▯ is up to satisfying property ▯.
  uakci (2023-01-03): a much more useful redefinition of **foa** that doesn't artificially separate the mood-haver x1 from the mood property x2. if you're having trouble understanding this definition: it's like **ıq** but it says that this property is a mood or state that you're going through
  uakci (2023-01-03): notably, **foa gı** and **foa huı** now mean something else (sth like ‘being (morally?) good is my mood’) and you'll have to use **gıfoa**, **huıfoa** instead. but also it lets you say **foa nuokuaı** 'be in a state of mind of feeling sleepy', **foa doekaı** 'be down for some programming / be in the mood/zone for programming', etc.
  uakci (2023-03-12): some notes about the notes: (1) **foa bâq gı** also works; (2) since this **foa** is a hyponym of **ıq**, this implies that one who **foa doekaı**s is already one who **doekaı**s. you can say **foa taoshao sạdoekaı** for ‘feel like you’re down for some programming’, whose compositionality i like. i suppose that in this sense **foa** is just like **taq moe ıq**

⚠ g @Laqme 2025-03-30 #bvr8NpVm6 $en +1
clitic: make the next word not attach to incorporated/predicatizer objects
  Laqme (2025-03-30): This is a sort of revival of Gamma's **ga** terminator. It's pronounced /gə/ with a very short neutral-tone vowel.
  Laqme (2025-03-30): After a phrase like **sá paı kê naq**, you can use **g duagı** to adjectivally modify "paı", or **g ꝡë** to attach a relative clause to "paı", or **g róı** to conjoin that whole DP with another DP.
  Laqme (2025-03-30): **sá kue po báq foto de g sao** "a big book of beautiful photos"
  Laqme (2025-03-30): I gave this experimental word an experimental form because I feel bad taking CVs, but I'd be just as happy with this as **ga**.

ga- @uakci 2023-11-01 #ZV2R-s68D $en ±
prefix: combines previous word X and next (attached) word Y into the compound YX, expressed as two parts for clarity or when forming an ad-hoc compound
  uakci (2023-11-01): note that not all compounds can use **gạ-** – only those which the speaker deems are a compound of two other existing lexical items can. so **marao** cannot be **rao gạkma** (unless the speaker really thinks that’s the etymology of **marao**. i don’t judge)
  uakci (2023-11-01): **rao gạma** (misspelling)
  uakci (2023-11-01): one other thing this prefix is nice for (apart from ad-hoc-ing compounds and decomposing existing compounds for the benefit of the listener) is breaking down an enormous word into two tone contours

gâ @Hoemai 2025-03-07 #a2boFbpIg $en ±
pendent particle: allows placing an argument in front and outside of a phrase in order to bind a variable within that phrase
  Hoemai (2025-03-07): Originally I thought that this would just be an empty preposition and therefore precede its complement. But this would require a change to the rules governing adjunct placement and scope, because pendent constructions often appear between subject and object (see examples below), and it would also limit its application.
  Hoemai (2025-03-07): I also seem to prefer the feel of **gâ** as a postposition. Given these points, I propose that **gâ** be like a barnacle.
  Hoemai (2025-03-07): Like a barnacle, it sits on top of whatever (phrase) is right next to it; its host is actually its complement, and the pendent argument itself is the specifier.
  Hoemai (2025-03-07): Example usage: **Shoe jí, sía raı gâ, ꝡä kuq súq ráı.** "I allow, for no X, that you say X." **Dua jí, sá poq gâ, ꝡä cho hó súq.** "I know, of someone, that they like you."
  Hoemai (2025-03-07): Based on Eatoaq's **nâ**, but keeps fronting-**nä** and pendent-**gâ** syntactically and phonologically distinct.

gaq- @uakci 2023-10-16 #JTZI3uEyi $en +1
prefix: split the subject into two arguments, which are then **róı**d together: ▯ together with ▯ collectively do X.
  uakci (2023-10-16): this is like the use of ‘with’ (or sometimes null) with some plural intransitive verbs in english: “we had a feud” ~ “i had a feud with them”; “we cuddled” ~ “i cuddled [with] them”
  fagri (2023-10-16): +1
  fagri (2023-10-16): This prefix suggests a paradigm where words like «maıgaq» are rephrased from having two slots to having one slot, which is something I've liked the idea of for a while. The current definitions could be recovered with this prefix when they're needed
  fagri (2023-10-16): «Maıgaq ı́me» = «Gạqmaıgaq jı́ nháo»

gaq [(c c) (d d) tá I] @robintown 2023-02-05 #5n4Dw0Jzw $en +3
▯ accompanies / is together with ▯.
  robintown (2023-02-05): Subject-sharing adverbials allow us to do away with the property place that this word has previously had.

gınu @uakci 2021-07-31 #mVB1pcsn4 $en ±
▯ is fortunately/fortuitously/happily/[luckily] so.
  uakci (2021-07-31): intended to be used in 7th tone, just like **shuaınū**, e.g., Shảı rủqshūa gĩnū da ‘Luckily, it stopped raining’. ‘luckily’ is meant here in a narrow sense – not as an indication of good luck / favourable odds, but as a “polarising” comment explaining the speaker’s positive bias towards the event being described

abbreviation of **gö** **jıjıe**/**jeokuq** **jí** **hóa** "in my (honest) opinion / IMO / IMHO"
  evie (2024-11-14): kw: textspeak

go- [  particle ] @uakci 2023-08-12 #8N9cUaefr $en +3
prefix: combine two verbs into a verb–object compound where the action denoted by the verb is specialized by the class of object
  uakci (2023-08-12): in other words, **A gọ-B** is a kind of **A** specific to **B**-s. for example, if A = ‘to sit sb’ and B = ‘baby’, then A **gọ-**B would mean babysitting, or the kind of sitting that’s specific to babies
  uakci (2023-08-12): note: the verb retains its original valency
  fagri (2023-11-25): +1
  seaqtaq (2024-07-16): Keyword: Semantic focuser/classifier

gö @Laqme 2022-12-19 #XXS8Vt-RQ $en +1
retroactive CP cleft: wraps the preceding sentence in the following CP
  Laqme (2022-12-19): https://toaq.net/refgram/syntax/#retroactive_cp_cleft
  Laqme (2022-12-19): **Kaqgaı súq báq aıpu, gö bu chı jí hóa.** = [that] You saw a ghost, I don't believe (that).

gö @official 2026-02-18 #BLd8__dsNjP $en ±
retroactive cleft: ‘RETROCLE’; lift top-level clause, binding hóa in the following matrix clause

gö @robintown 2024-07-31 #l02wTUAmv $en ±
cleft verb: opens incidental clause commenting on the complement of the speech act
  robintown (2024-07-31): Binds the complement of the sentence's speech act to hóa.
  robintown (2024-07-31): De báq rua, gö dua tú poq hóa. "Flowers are beautiful, as everyone knows."
  robintown (2024-07-31): Hoaı mıe nháo, gö chı jí hóa. "He's still alive—I think."
  robintown (2024-07-31): Might even have free placement similar to vocatives: Jaq suao, gö jıe jí hóa, lä sẹuleo já hú. "It's very important, I find, to at least try it."

gohı @Ilmen 2022-10-07 #d8jpU-R0R $en ± %logic
The proposition "what satisfies the predicate ▯" is such that ▯ is in relation ▯ with that proposition. More precisely: considering the predicate ▯, the thing ▯ and the relation ▯: for all X, the proposition that X is an argument structure which satisfies the predicate is true iff the thing is in that relation with the proposition.
  Ilmen (2022-10-07): Example: «gỏhı dủa jí jîa lủaqtaı ja da» = «dủa jí jîa lủaqtaı hı da».

guoı [(c 2xx) (n d) tá F] @uakci 2026-02-17 #l3eEYuQEs $en ±
▯ are two things standing on both sides of relation ▯; ▯ are two things, one of which satisfies relation ▯ with the other; ▯ are an “X-er and X-ed”, where X is the relation ▯.
  uakci (2026-02-17): < **gu** + **cuoı**. example: **guoı pao** ‘to be parent and child’ → **gúoı pao** ‘the parent and their child’
  uakci (2026-02-17): for symmetrical relationships, this boils down to **gu cheo**: **guoı paı** ‘to be two friends’ = **gu cheo paı**. asymmetrical examples: **guoı cheom** ‘sinner and their sin’; **guoı muı** ‘[text] and its meaning’; **guoı hıaoja** ‘perpetrator and victim’
  uakci (2026-02-17): given the usual mathematical treatment of relations as subsets of U × U (where U is the domain/universe), this **guoı** expresses membership modulo ordering: “the unordered tuple {a, b} for a ≠ b is s.t. aRb or bRa”

guoı [(c 2xx) (n d) tá F] @uakci 2026-02-17 #p1Ouo8RA5 $toa ±
jeı sá shı róı sá nọshı já gu rú cuoı ra bocuoı shí nọ́shı já guja

gúom @uakci 2024-12-31 #vkH9iauhO $en +1
focus particle: more (of) X; X more/further; X again/still; X anew; another X / X yet another time
  uakci (2024-12-31): **gıshe, ꝡä bạqjoımoı báq cueche gúom báq mıa jıe** ‘politicians should take public opinion into consideration more’ vs. **gıshe, ꝡä bạqjoımoı gúom báq cueche báq mıa jıe**. in both cases, the speaker wishes for the **ꝡä** clause to happen more (further than already), but in the first case they want more/further/new/other public opinion to be considered and in the second case they want a wider range of politicians (so, including more/further/new/different politicians than already do) to consider public opinion
  uakci (2024-12-31): you can also probably save yourself the nuance and slap **guom** at the start of the clause: **gıshe, ꝡä guom joımoı** etc. etc.

guroı @uakci 2019-08-24 #hXQG0WWic $en ±
▯ is such a proposition that its first slot argument together (roı) with ▯ satisfies it with respect to the first slot; If ▯ = ⟨P(xx, …)⟩, then ⊢ P(xx ∪ ▯, …).
  uakci (2019-08-24): in the sixth tone: Jảq ní lî sảo na gùrōı níjāo — This is very big together with that = Jảq ní ru níjāo lî sảo
  uakci (2019-08-24): as a serial head: Gủrōı fả jí sóaq ta hó — I (we) went to the garden with them = Fả jí roı hó sóaq
  uakci (2019-08-24): possibly useful for completing a cheo-ish clause semantically in afterthought
  robintown (2024-09-07): We would normally use **gâq** for the second example nowadays

  Ilmen (2025-10-31): officialized: yes

predicate: ‘generally’; ▯ is generally the case; ▯ is how things generally are when ▯ is the case. (gnomic aspect)
  Machy (2020-06-30): credit to hoaqgio
  fagri (2020-07-06): I pass the credit on to Hoemai, ka

hê @Laqme 2023-07-17 #T89rH7hLr $en ±
gnomic conditional: **hê, ꝡä X, nä Y** = "As a rule, whenever X is the case, Y is the case."
  Laqme (2023-07-17): (Hoemaı, January 16, 2023) “he is not really an aspect, but I couldn't be arsed to figure out where to put it in the refgram so I left it where it used to be. It belongs to the Modal/AdverbOfQuantification group of words, which have two places (restriction & scope).”
  Laqme (2023-07-17): source: https://discord.com/channels/311223912044167168/889589074011230230/1064656781848092693
  Laqme (2023-07-17): Not really lexical tone — if you see "hê", that means the speaker is using **he** as a modal like **daı** or **ao**, rather than an aspect like **chum** or **luı**.

heomaı [(c) (d) hó I] @fagri 2019-06-30 #6xy9DLG51 $en -1
▯ is straight (attracted to people of the opposite sex)
  fagri (2021-08-02): (includes bisexual people I think)
  maigoi (2024-09-17): that tells me 'loves what is different', could as well be a xenophile or someone who likes to explore new, different things than they already know
  maigoi (2024-09-18): there should be a shorter root for ( ) is 'romantically' & 'sexually' 'attracted' to ( ) for use instead of mai
  mimiaoganhaqpy (2024-09-18): Hi!  I personally think this word is fine as is because the word “gay” for example, could mean that someone is homoromantic or homosexual.  There can also exist less specific words.  Of course, more specific words are always welcome, but I think most people would rather say “I am straight” than “I am heteroromantic and heterosexual.”
  mimiaoganhaqpy (2024-09-18): As for your note that this word doesn’t mention gender anywhere, the alternative would be heojeqda[mai/romantic-attract/sexual-attract]. You could think of this word as an shortening of that word. Additionally, Toaq compounds are not always just their parts combined. For example, choıshoaı means hawk, but it is a compound word “sharp-wing”
  mimiaoganhaqpy (2024-09-18): Oh, and @maigoi, the word **heoseaq** exists for heterosexual.
  mimiaoganhaqpy (2024-09-18): Additionally, you are free to coin new words as you like.  Most words in Toaq are created by the community based on need.  For example, this word was coined by @fagri, not Hoemai (the creator of Toaq).  So you are more than welcome to create a word for heteromantic :)
  mimiaoganhaqpy (2024-09-18): You can join us on our Discord server https://discord.gg/Wxn6XF4G to discuss coining words.
  maigoi (2024-09-22): hello! I like non-specifity! I just mean the word is indeed nice, but evokes a broader meaning than specifically heterosexuality :)

I think the definition itself could be more... interesting;

now, I don't think there would need to be a compound of three roots, just a new root for the two of them which are very common!

I have no creativity for creating roots and I made a toki pona words generator, not a Toaq one so, I'm unlucky now xD

I like how the community creates its own words, but I'm seeing my way into Toaq for pure aesthetics, and I highly consider grammar (thus learning a logical language) and well-made compounds (so it doesn't make the grammar useless as 'logical'). those factors are important to me, as I wouldn't a logical language being harder to parse than toki pona, due to... autistic reasons ._.

I don't use Discord but suspect this is bridged;

▯ makes itself visible, is self-imposing, metaphorically shines

horabaq @fagri 2019-02-24 #R_xY20twN $en ±

horachıu @fagri 2019-02-24 #rOEf_3vR2 $en ± %time
▯ is the time ▯ (three- or four-digit number)
  fagri (2019-02-24): The entity named is the set of evenly spaced points in time that occur at time (h)h:mm
  fagri (2019-02-24): Example: "rào hórāchīu shỉgūgū" = "at 1:22 am", "rào hórāchīu gủhēısāqhēı" = "at 8:30 pm" (= 20:30)
  fagri (2019-02-24): Oh also, a one- or two-digit argument means that only the hour is being named, but it still refers to exactly a set of *points* in time; "rào hórāchīu hẻı" = "at 10:00 am (sharp)"

húaq @uakci 2024-09-13 #DfEZbQWcV $en ±
focus particle: the more X, the more …; specifies that it’s X whose extent/intensity contributes to the extent to which the containing clause holds
  uakci (2024-09-13): assumes experimental **sóq** as focus particle (which in that case displaces official **huaq** as **mẹ́asoq** together with **hạ-**)
  uakci (2024-09-13): lynn has already defined **hụaq-** the focus prefix but i think this is both close enough and more pragmatic (for intensification specifically there's also **ꝡạm-**)
  uakci (2024-09-13): **he jaı báq gaqcheo kıhụaqpuı** ≈ loosely ‘the more, the merrier’

ı- @TheAndSys 2025-02-18 #79nP7v3sm $en ±

ı @uakci 2023-01-01 #1Z1sroujR $en ±
▯ pertains to cyberspace.
  uakci (2023-01-01): mostly intended as a (formal or informal) prefix for turning meatspace activities into their cyberspace analogues, e.g., **ıtı jí íkua** ‘i’m present/logged into the chatroom’ (also **ịtı jí ̣
  uakci (2023-01-01): although **tı jí íkua** is probably fine too since the literal reading cannot be meant because of the category error
  uakci (2023-01-01): < english e- < electronic
  uakci (2023-01-01): choosing this word form because it gives compounds a highly distinctive sound
  uakci (2023-01-01): the first comment should read “also **ịtı jí ị́kua**”

ïe [  particle ] @Laqme 2025-01-02 #-ubuUiPzI $en +4
start editorial parenthetical (closed by **kı**)
  Laqme (2025-01-02): Used inside **mó** … **teo**. This indicates a parenthetical that was not in the actual quote. Inside the parenthetical, **hóa** refers to the quoted text.
  Laqme (2025-01-02): **Kuq nháo mó «Naına Tóeqzu (ïe nu hóa kı) ꝡeı» teo.**  They said, “Toeq [sic] is so cool.”
  Laqme (2025-01-02): And maybe if you use it outside of mó-teo, it lifts the sentence to a quote? **«Jaq jua,» ïe kuq mí Alısı hóa kı, «ní sıo da.»** "This idea," said Alice, "is quite strange."
  Ilmen (2025-10-23): This can presumably also be used for rendering switch to meta descriptions as seen from a third party, as in ⟪They still fail to understand. *facepalm*⟫.

⚠ ïgo @seaqtaq 2024-07-16 #wxeB-9cn1 $en ±
relative retroactive cleft: ‘RELRETROCLE’; lift superordinate clause, binding hóa in the following clause on the same level as said superordinate clause
  seaqtaq (2024-07-16): Intended to cover for a (perceived) weakness of gö—namely that it can only be used to add extra details to the main proposition, and not propositions represented in arbitrarily nested subordinate claues
  seaqtaq (2024-07-16): "Bu chı jí, ꝡá noaq nháo sá, ꝡä fa déo jéarıaq zûo pó báq noaı, ïgo bu le hóa"=I don't believe he's slowly [afterthought cleft] reading something about a boy going to the story
  seaqtaq (2024-07-16): Similarly, in "Bu chı jí, ꝡá noaq nháo sá, ꝡä fa déo jéarıaq zûo pó báq noaı, ïgo bu le hóa, ïgo kuı hôa sá bụtuao", the second ïgo esssentially functions as gö in this case since there are only three neating levels
  seaqtaq (2025-11-09): DEPRECIATED - INVALID WORD SHAPE. See **cạ-** for a word with a similar function

jeha [(0) (d) hóq P] @Hoemai 2022-08-06 #-MLQG4xTd $en +2
▯ is* the case.
  Hoemai (2022-08-06): This predicate expresses that its argument is true and that this disagrees with a proposition from the immediately preceding discourse (there should be one most salient proposition). Like German "doch" and French "si", but as a verb. If no argument is provided, it defaults to the negation of the most salient proposition.
  Hoemai (2022-08-06): A: **Bủ chỏ súq jí** B: **Jẻha chô jí súq**. A: "You don't like me." B: "Yes, I do like you."
  Hoemai (2022-08-06): A: **Bủ bỉaq dẻ jí** B: **Jẻha da**. A: "I'm not pretty enough." B: "Yes, (you are)."
  robintown (2022-10-25): See also **aımu**

jị̂aq- @uakci 2025-11-28 #4sopBa3KO $en +2
prefix: turn a modality into a complementizer adjoining to a noun phrase to resolve its referents to the possible-worlds predicated by the complementizer clause
  uakci (2025-11-28): **jí, jị̂aq'ao naq jí** ‘me if I was a man’; ‘me [in worlds in which I’d be a man]’; **zíudıu, jị̂aq'ea jeı péı 4 pízıu** ‘mathematics if pi could be 4’
  uakci (2025-11-28): (does not have to be used with a counterfactual modality; if not, then the resolved referents are ambiguous as to whether they belong to w0)
  uakci (2025-11-28): this word could see more colloquial use in the form of, say, **jí, jị̂aqshe shea nuokuaı jí** ≈‘me on days that i’m not tired’ or **jí, jị̂aqshe loe río** ≈‘me on warm days’

jıeqja @uakci 2023-11-14 #Fgg0WYzeZ $en ±
▯ brings ▯ (situation) to its edge/utmost.
  uakci (2023-11-14): **taocıa jıeqja roq nháo jí** ‘they unwittingly got me to tear up’, implying that the ‘tearing up’ reached its peak and then presumably receded afterwards. this is an aspectual distinction (X happening vs. X peaking) that’s not so easy to express in english so take my word for it. cf. **jıeqla**

jıeqkıoqbıa @uakci 2021-08-19 #xZ-4v7raK $en ±
▯ is sick with the coronavirus.
  uakci (2021-08-19): Latin ‘corona’ refers to a crown, specifically to its characteristic ring of pointed details that it has. a “coronavirus” is any virus which takes the shape of a sphere that is peppered with extremities

jıeqla @uakci 2023-11-14 #fdbWgoU0K $en ±
▯ (situation) peaks/climaxes / reaches its apex (in terms of extent/intensity).
  uakci (2023-11-14): **kú pûcham nä jıeqla jaı jí** would mean more or less ‘my happiness peaked yesterday’
  uakci (2023-11-14): this word expresses a rather nuanced aspectual distinction. when **nuokuaı jí** means ‘i was sleepy’, **jıeqla nuokuaı jí** could be translated unwieldily as ‘i got sleepier and sleepier until reaching a peak/extreme of sleepiness [and then it presumably got better. idk maybe i napped or maybe the urge abated a little]’

jıoq [(c 0) (d d) hó A] @uakci 2021-11-06 #YFQwzEYEH $en +2 %cognition
▯ calculates ▯ [see notes].
  uakci (2021-11-06): this is a reformulation of <@official jıoq> that avoids depending on properties as answer-givers. x₂ may be filled with a proposition or an indirect question
  uakci (2021-11-06): if x₂ is a (non-interrogative) proposition, the verb means “to calculate that x₂ is the case”; “to come to the conclusion that x₂ is the case through calculation/reckoning”
  uakci (2021-11-06): ex.: **jıoq jí pêu péı jo péı gu péı gu** – ‘I calculate that 2 + 2 = 4’
  uakci (2021-11-06): this form may be used in a serial (**jıoq peu jí péı jo péı gu péı gu** = the same); moreover, you can say **jıoq peu jí** ‘I add’ (I calculate addition operations)
  uakci (2021-11-06): if x₂ is a polar interrogative proposition (one with **ma**), this verb means “to verify if x₂ is the case”. x₂ is not asserted
  uakci (2021-11-06): if x₂ is an interrogative proposition involving **tıo**, **co**, **hı**, this verb can be translated like the official definition – x₁ calculates what the answer to x₂ is
  Ilmen (2025-12-02): ⟪jıoq⟫ is for when you search for the solution to a question sheerly by mental manipulations of already known data.
  Dembim (2026-02-11): Frame: c 0

jıoq [(c 0) (d d) hó A] @fagri 2023-06-13 #IQzrU8uBm $en +2 %cognition

  fagri (2023-06-13): «Jıoq séoqtuedıupoq mä ruqshua râo jı́achaq» = "The meteorologists calculated whether it will rain tomorrow"
  fagri (2023-06-13): «Jıoq nháo wä reu hı́ shı́ko gúko» = "They calculated the product of the first and second number"
  Ilmen (2025-12-02): ⟪jıoq⟫ is for when you search for the solution to a question sheerly by mental manipulations of already known data.

jıq- @uakci 2023-06-07 #FkNtiXb-C $en ±
prefix: given a verb X expressing a denomination of date or time, form a new verb meaning “▯ is the X described by the following number-expressions, where the first number-expression word is the denomination of X and each following word is one denomination higher”
  uakci (2023-06-07): < **jıoq**. examples: **jịqchaq 15 2 2021** = ‘the 15th [day] of the 2nd [month] of the 2021st [year]’, or 2021-02-15; **jịqmınu 31 13** = ‘the 31st [minute] of the 13th [month]’, or 13:31
  uakci (2023-06-07): to clarify, these are pronounced like regular™ numbers (except that i assume you may omit **heı**, etc.), so the first example would be fully spelled out and pronounced as **jịqchaq shıfe gu gukoamgushı**
  uakci (2023-06-07): i meant hour not month in that second example btw. also, these units may be mixed date–time, e.g., **jịqhora 23 27** ‘23:xx on the 27th’

ka- @Ilmen 2025-12-07 #Q7srdSp3q $en +1
prefix: prefixed to a number root, it turns it into a special word meaning ⟪▯ is identified as the [number]th thing with property ▯ referenced in current C-command syntactic context⟫; its use is to disambiguate variables or clauses which would otherwise bear the same name: ⟪kaqgaı sá kato sá kato⟫ → ⟪kaqgaı sá (kạshı) kato sá kạgu kato⟫ “some cat see a second cat”, allowing referring back to the second cat as ⟪kạ́gu⟫ or ⟪kạ́gu kato⟫ subsequently in the same clause.

kalo @jiqci 2023-06-22 #tYmiprJP2 $en ±
▯ is a calorie/kilocalorie.
  jiqci (2023-06-22): there were historically two calories, the large calorie being the amount of energy required to heat 1kg of water by 1 degree, and the small calorie being 1/1000th of that, i.e. heat required for 1-degree change of 1g of water. The term "calorie" nowadays refers to the large calorie, while "kilocalorie" means 1000 of the small calories, i.e. the two are equivalent.

kaqjeo @fagri 2019-01-09 #ylRRH91wB $en ±
▯ satisfies property ▯, which describes something about how it/they look
  fagri (2019-01-09): For example, "Kảqjēo hó lî jảqjēq shẻaqsāo ja dó báq mủao da", she was as tall as a tree.
  fagri (2019-01-09): On the other hand "Kảqjēo hó lî jải ja dó" is NOT true even if "Jải hó" is true, because "lî jải ja dó is not a property describing how dó looks
  fagri (2019-01-09): This word is useful to form phrases like "I don't know what she looks like".  (Kảqsīu, "▯ looks like ▯" would not be appropriate, because that's a comparison between two concretes)
  fagri (2019-01-09): Oh, and I just thought of "jêo hó hi káq", so consider that too before using this word; it might be better.
  uakci (2023-07-28): (→ **kaq'ıq**)

ku'aı @seaqtaq 2020-07-03 #Twooh8oMY $en ±
'orientation'; ▯ʼs orientation in aspect/relevant to property ▯  is described by property ▯
  seaqtaq (2020-07-03): Word form inspired by "kuaı". The second place relates to a scale and/or collection of beliefs or behaviors and the third place describes exactly what behaviors or beliefs the referent of the first place exhibits, or where they are on the scale
  seaqtaq (2020-07-03): "Kủxāı súq lî củe ru cảq ja baq jủaocīao hı moq?"—"What is your political orientation" (Or "Please describe what properties best describe your beliefs/behaviors as relevant to ruling and managing societies")
  seaqtaq (2020-07-03): This bisyllabic root is purposefully very broad and as such can be used to name sexual, gender, political, etc. orientation
  salarua (2022-09-13): word form is no longer distinct from kuai

lı [(c 2xi) (n d) tá F] @uakci 2023-12-02 #phXJYoHPd $en +1
As a general rule, one satisfies, with ▯, relation ▯; [≈ **he** **ıqha**]
  uakci (2023-12-02): **he** often goes with **há**, so understand **lı** as an abbreviation of this pattern: **he huaqcho há báq kırı** ‘one usually prefers cute things’ = **lı huaqcho báq kırı** ‘cute things are preferred’
  uakci (2023-12-02): syntactic aside: since **lı** has a built-in **ha** Asp node, i’d like to treat **lı** as the masked spelled-out form of an Asp node above and a V node below, namely **he** and **ıqha**
  uakci (2023-12-02): (in other words, some ✨automagical syntactic process✨ turns the continuous sequence **he ıqha** into **lı**, perhaps during Spell-Out, the syntactic phase infamous for having all kinds of magic shoved into it

▯ is a sign/indication of ▯.
  Laqme (2024-09-29): symptom, evidence
  Laqme (2024-09-29): I'm splitting an accidental sense of **laı** out of that word so that that word can just mean "glyph, letter".
  Laqme (2024-09-29): This makes for a nice adverb: **lîam, ꝡä ...** = indicating that / showing that ... is the case.
  Laqme (2024-09-29): **Lıam, é ruı púao, ꝡä fịruqshua** = The clouds being gray is a sign that it's about to rain.
  Laqme (2024-09-29): **Lıam, é geı jí báq gıaqchea, ꝡä shao sịakeoı jí** = Me wearing headphones is a sign that I want to not talk to anyone.
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Frame: c 0
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Pronominal_class: hoq
  Dembim (2026-02-09): kw: suggests, diagnostic

▯ follows / matches / obeys / goes along / is in accordance with ▯.
  Laqme (2024-05-18): This is a lot like *tiao*, and almost equally generic, but the object sets a tone/example/lead/constraint and the subject follows or fulfills it
  Laqme (2024-05-18): A path "follows" a river, proper behavior "follows" the rules, an improvised melody "follows" the chords, mountain ranges "follow" tectonic plates, data "follows" a pattern, files "follow" a file format, an artwork "follows" an aesthetic or movement, you "follow" your tango dance partner
  seaqtaq (2024-11-14): Conform
  lassc (2025-03-12): acquiescence
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Frame: c c
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Pronominal_class: ta
  lassc (2026-03-15): "congruent/congruence", "in line with", "in the character of", "in agreement with" (only in some senses)

lıpoe @fagri 2019-12-25 #fvr0hGlXw $en ±
What satisfies property ▯ is independent of what satisfies property ▯
  fagri (2019-12-25): In other words, there are no ordered pairs (A, B) that are forbidden from satisfying the properties (P1, P2), unless A is always forbidden from satisfying P1 or B is always forbidden from satisfying P2
  fagri (2019-12-25): Another way of saying the same thing is that the ordered pairs (A, B) that can satisfy (P1, P2) are simply the cross product of the values A that can satisfy P1 and the values B that can satisfy P2
  fagri (2019-12-25): This word only kind of came up in a real context and might turn out to be designed from too high an ivory tower to be useful

[PO] property ▯ is satisfied by [complement]
  fagri (2019-02-18): A single-arg applicator in PO.  Example: "Lóao jí ga pảı" = "Lú pảı jí hóa na".

loeq [(c)  tá ] @uakci 2022-12-02 #-E6dKlMwd $en +1
▯ cooks/is cooked.
  uakci (2022-12-02): replaces **loy**, **lony**
  uakci (2022-12-02): boils, boiled; cooks, cooked; fries, fried; steams, steamed; treats, treated; pickles, pickled; ferments, fermented; ages, aged; smokes, smoked; kneads, kneaded
  uakci (2022-12-02): (not all of these glosses make sense as unaccusatives but including them for completeness
  uakci (2022-12-02): bakes, baked; ovens, ovened; marinates, marinated; sautées, sautéed
  uakci (2022-12-02): spoils, spoiled; rots, rotten; molds/moulds, moldy/molded/mouldy/moulded
  uakci (2024-03-11): this is an unaccusative verb, meaning that **loeq kásı** means ‘the nuts cook/are cooking’ and not ‘the nuts cook themselves’ or ‘the nuts are cooked by somebody’ (although this could still be implied). use **tua loeq** for transitive ‘cook’, e.g., **tua loeq jí kásı** ‘i cook the nuts’. also, this word expresses the action of cooking – becoming cooked – and not the state of having been cooked (so possibly ready to eat) – for that, use **luı loeq**, or its abbreviated variant **loq'e**
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Frame: c
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Pronominal_class: ta
  Dembim (2026-02-12): I prefer that this word _not_ include the various ways something can go foul. The result of <loeq> should be something edible.

meq- @uakci 2023-10-13 #5lDBV3chi $en ±
prefix: turn verb into focus prefix
  uakci (2023-10-13): see **meq**, **kueu**. the mechanics of this word goes as follows: •if X is binary, then the focus prefix feeds the focused part of a proposition as the first argument and the ‘remainder’ expressed as a lambda as the second argument. for example, **cho jí mẹ́qsoq súq** transforms into **soq súq, lä cho jí já**. in this vein, **mẹ́q'ıq** corresponds to the no-op focus prefix. note that the original preposition is lifted, i.e., no longer asserted (in favor of the ancillary proposition whose predicate is X); •if X is unary, then the use of the focus prefix is equivalent to an adjoined clause with it replaced by **kú** – for example, **cho jí mẹ́qgıfaq súq** transforms into **cho jí súq; gıfaq, ꝡä cho jî kú súq**. note that this use is essentially similar to an eventive adverb (here, **gîfaq**), so like a focused/scoped adverbial if you will

meq @Ilmen 2020-01-10 #RSxfBglvT $en +1
quantifier: the proposition in which this quantifier appears is treated as a property with a lambda slot placed exactly where this quantifier appears; the resulting property is then claimed to satisfy the first slot of the predicate introduced by this quantifier.
  Ilmen (2020-01-10): ``mủıdūa meq nỏa ní`` = ``nỏa mûıdūa ja ní``.
  Ilmen (2020-01-14): ``meq hảo`` is similar to ``hão`` but instead of delivering a proposition (lû) to the first slot of ``hao`` as is the case with the latter, it passes a property (lî) to it. Compare ``chỏq jí ní jẽaq dảqfāı`` —"I use this increasingly frequently"— with ``chỏq meq pủıjēaq ní`` —"increasingly many ones use this"—.
  Ilmen (2020-01-14): What can be expressed as ``gủaı meq dùq póq`` under the alternative definition #46CV57Aje can be expressed as per this very definition as ``gủaı póq ru meq hỏpā dủq``, which is three syllables more. Hopefully the additional flexibility allowed by this one definition is a sufficient compensation.

mẹ́qjoq'ıq @uakci 2024-01-07 #Rz5ut7I-m $en ±
focus marker: primarily, predominantly, mainly, chiefly, overwhelmingly, as a majority (of cases / of concern)

mı- [  particle ] @uakci 2023-06-03 #NAS9wtQdz $en +2
prefix: turns the following verb X into a name marker that expects a word «Y» and turns it into the verb: ▯ is an X named «Y».
  uakci (2023-06-03): example: **mịhıu Lue** ‘the river Yellow’
  uakci (2023-06-03): the definition is a little silly and hard to follow so think of the word this way: it takes a verb and produces a specialized **mı**. so **mịhıu Lue** ‘to be a river named Yellow’ is a specialization of **mı Lue** ‘to be [something] named Yellow’

mı @uakci 2023-06-03 #ioiqJZ3A0 $en +1
name verb: ‘NAME’; turns following word X into verb: ▯ is called X; ▯ is something satisfying property ▯ that is called X.
  uakci (2023-06-03): this pans out better for names with “classifiers” like **mı Lue hıu** ‘the Yellow River’ or **mı Alesaqdo suoı** ‘Alexander the Great’

  Isi (2024-09-06): Then **chua** would be used for the predicate “▯ is called X”.

mımoe @uakci 2022-06-20 #wo56vtLG- $en ±
onomatopoeic/mimetic quote: ▯ goes X (as in, displays behaviour or state of mind or feeling that “rattles” like X; not necessarily that they *say* X out loud).
  uakci (2022-06-20): apt for the entire range of sound-symbolic vocabulary out there (from phonomime to phenomime to psychomime); can be used to borrow mimetic vocabulary from other languages on the spot; also intended to be used with interjections, as in **mımoe oro** ‘to be like ugh’ ≈ ‘to be (visibly) exasperated’
  uakci (2022-06-20): this particle is in SHU and thus accepts one word of any form
  uakci (2022-06-20): etymology: ‘mimetic’ + **moe**. i guess you could say it’s **mı** + **moe** too, same difference
  uakci (2022-06-20): the word was at first intended to be able to capture and reuse interjections as verbs (as in, to be able to say things like “he ughed”), but on second thoughts it turned out to be much more versatile than that
  uakci (2022-06-20): ≈ **aı shủ …**, **aı mỏ … teo** but more on-point and cuter
  Snow (2023-05-24): keyword: onomatopoeia

molem [(c 0) (d d) hó I] @magnap 2024-09-26 #8HICOI_WT $en +2
▯D/c holds in their mind the possibilities-inherent-in-▯N/0 (declarative *or* interrogative clause); from Estonian "mõtlema"
  magnap (2024-09-26): in a declarative clause there is only one possibility to hold in your mind, and so this means something akin to "believes"

nạ́'ao @salarua 2024-10-05 #xNXVz1ZJR $en +1
conjunction: except
  salarua (2024-10-05): X nạ́'ao Y denotes that a statement would be true for X if it were true for Y; i.e. Y is an exception to the statement being true
  salarua (2024-10-05): "Tı tú mea póqme jıbo nạ́'ao mí Moıra géqme da" = "Everyone in my group except Moira was at the meeting"
  salarua (2024-10-05): Or more literally: "'Everyone in my group was at the meeting' would have been true if Moira had been at the meeting"

particle: discourse-level frame-setting cleft — placed after a tense or modal expression, setting it as the domain in which the following sentences are evaluated as true, until the next occurrence of ⟪näꝡa⟫. no- @uakci 2022-12-21 #qMYho2EIa $en +1
prefix: [identity prefix]; X
  uakci (2022-12-21): h/t @Laqme. intended to be used in situations where two bindings would otherwise clash – an extra **no-** at the front of one of those bindings distinguishes them nominally. e.g., **he ꝡä maı báq poq báq nọpoq shê ꝡä maı nópoq póq**, 'a person usually loves another person if the other person loves that person'. notice how **no-** translates to '(an)other' in this example

particle: discourse-level frame-setting cleft — placed after a tense or modal expression, setting it as the domain in which the following sentences are evaluated as true, until the next occurrence of this particle. nubo @uakci 2019-04-12 #Fi0WykEbN $en +1
▯ is such as ▯; ▯ satisfies the propertie(s) which ▯ is characterised (characterized) to satisfy.
  uakci (2019-04-12): Explanation: we take the x2 and look at what properties it’s typically associated with; then, we assert that x1 has them. For example: **nủbō ní bủajīo báq lỏe**, if flames are known to be hot, bright, and burning, would assert that that building is hot, bright, and burning.

num [(c c) (d d) tá F] @Laqme 2025-05-11 #JMGiPbFSd $en +3
▯ involves ▯.
  Laqme (2025-05-11): Also: features / uses / takes / is powered by / runs on / depends on.
  Laqme (2025-05-11): A bit like **choq** without agency, or like **chıa** without the vibe of obligation. The intent is for **nûm** to be a quite generically applicable preposition ("involving").
  Laqme (2025-05-11): Math *involves* numbers, Toaq *features* serial verbs, this recipe *uses* soy sauce and a wok, love *takes* courage, computers *run on* electricity, and (crucially) events *involve* all their participants.
  Laqme (2025-05-11): Probably about as generic as **bo**.
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Frame: c c
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Pronominal_class: ta

  Dembim (2026-02-10): <chuedoem> (public transport), <ceoqkoaı> (electrical grid), <chaohıu> (motorways), hospital systems, sanitation, wastewater management, communication networks, etc.
  Dembim (2026-02-10): Frame: c
  Dembim (2026-02-10): Pronominal_class: maq
  Dembim (2026-02-10): Can also include shelters, park systems, educational centers, or any other human-centric projects insofar as they are necessary to maintain a given scale of society.

numero @xorxes 2021-08-27 #I3Lozgsc_ $en ±
▯is/are a number of thing(s) satisfying property▯.
  xorxes (2021-08-27): **Numērō shamū sa saq shamū da.** 'Three apples are a number of apples'
  xorxes (2021-08-27): **Numērō baq saq da.** 'Three is (are) a number.'
  xorxes (2021-08-27): **Ma numērō shamū sa gu ko saq paq shamū moq** 'Are two thirds of an apple a number of apples?'
  xorxes (2021-08-27): **Ma numērō shamū sa coq shamū moq?** 'Are zero apples a number of apples?'

nhebıo @Dembim 2026-01-29 #U63mt3AsN $en ±
volume unit: cup (see notes)
  Dembim (2026-01-29): What means "a cup" can be a deeply messy affair. Everyone has their own version of every basic culinary measure. As much as I would like to imagine that Toaqgua is immune to such messiness, as a matter of cold reality, words for real world units are only useful if they accord with the real world. So, this word is for the _contextual_ cup, whatever a cup means to the speaker in the place and time that they speak. One should use the below, more specific words if further precision is required.
  Dembim (2026-01-29): <usonhebıo>, <gomonhebıo>, <nuınhebıo>, <fuınhebıo>
  Dembim (2026-01-29): Etymology: lexicalization of <@uakci nhe-> + <@official bıo>

nhu- @Neaqbua 2024-10-02 #hYS_LAmyo $en ±
prefix: Names or refers to a variable like **nha-**, but otherwise behaves like the verb on its own, including variable binding.
  Neaqbua (2024-10-02): 	For example, when you say ‹nhú-kune› for the first time (or ‹**nhúaı-**kune›), it binds ‹kúne›, ‹hó›, and ‹nhú-kune›. Then, saying ‹nhú-kune› will bring up the original referent, which again binds ‹kúne› and ‹hó›.
  Neaqbua (2024-10-02): 	Intuitively, ‹nhú-kune› is read as "a dog that will remain relevant for a while" or "that dog from before."
  Neaqbua (2024-10-02): 	‹nhu-X› can only be rebound by ‹**nhuaı-**X›. For clarity in long texts, you can introduce new ‹nhu-X› bindings always with ‹**nhuaı**-X›.
  Neaqbua (2024-10-02): 	In contrast to **nha-**, the verb is not arbitrary: ‹nhú-raımı›, like ‹ráımı›, is restricted by the "raımı(_)" predicate.

nhu @official 2026-02-18 #-ylnSC_k_KA $en ±
sentence connector: ‘anyway’; return to old topic

nhuaı- @Neaqbua 2024-10-02 #S_5mEGPtU $en ±
prefix: Like **nhu-**, but always names a new variable, overriding previous ‹nhu-X› bindings.
  Neaqbua (2024-10-02): 	Introduces something relevant in the current context without worrying about previous bindings to the same word. See **nhu-**.
  Neaqbua (2024-10-02): 	Intuitively, ‹nhúaı-kune› is read as "a dog that will remain relevant for a while."

nhuq'ao @uakci 2023-06-04 #XgJn3l2hI $en ±
▯ acts as a proxy/lackey/intermediary of ▯; ▯ does something for ▯ so they don’t have to do it themselves.
  uakci (2023-06-04): use as a subject-sharing adverbial
  uakci (2023-06-04): this is to specialize **nhuq** when it’s not really meant as a benefactive but as part of a separate frame (one of replacement/delegation). so **dıeq jí júo nhûq súq nha** ‘i’ll send the letter in your favor’ could be saying ‘i’ll send the letter to you’ (though you should use **com** or similar here) whereas **dıeq jí júo nhûq'ao súq nha** ‘i’ll send the letter in lieu of you [doing it yourself]’

pa- @uakci 2024-03-07 #RunxMd75v $en -1
prefix: pre-fill the subject of the marked matrix clause verb with **súq** and require **ba** illocution (which may then be omitted, as ∅)

pa @el 2022-10-20 #6GYakxr1u $en -1
argument fronter: takes a single argument (not an adverbial) to its left, and the verbal complex to its right; inserts the left argument between the verb and the first argument on the right
  el (2022-10-20): must appear *after* all **bı**s; cannot be nested
  el (2022-10-20): gritty details: **pa** is a pronounced F head(!) that generates with a VP complement (like current FPs) but also with an empty specifier (unlike them); Spec,V is moved out and into Spec,F, leaving the V' node intact and unmoved
  el (2022-10-20): note that this means that sentences like **jí pa gı da**, with a unary matrix verb, are ungrammatical
  el (2022-10-20): **jí pa fả jéarıaq**: [SAP [CP [C Ø] [FP Ø_1 [F' [F pa] [VP [DP jí<1>] [V' [V fa] [DP [D t2] [V jeariaq]]]]]]] [SA Ø]]
  el (2022-10-20): with the DP marked <1> moving up to the node marked _1
  Hoemai (2022-10-20): Looks like this (slightly adjusted): https://dprebyl.github.io/syntree/#%5BSAP%20%5BCP%20%5BC%20%C3%98%5D%20%5BFP%20%5BDP_1%20j%C3%AD%5D%20%5BF'%20%5BF%20pa%5D%20%5BVP%20%5BDP%20t%3C1%3E%5D%20%5BV'%20%5BV%20fa%5D%20%5BDP%20%5BD%20t2%5D%20%5BV%20jeariaq%5D%5D%5D%5D%5D%5D%5D%20%5BSA%20%C3%98%5D%5D

pá [  particle ] @Laqme 2022-08-17 #f_T-L2FKt $en +2
illocution marker: imperative with elided súq subject
  Laqme (2022-08-17): for example **Kaqsı jí pá!** = "look at me!". the subject DP had moved into SA, leaving a trace that makes the sentence "VO"
  Laqme (2022-08-17): I just think súq...bâ imperatives aren't very snappy

rara [(0) (d) hóq P] @Laqme 2019-01-25 #-W0QS1OdY $en +4
▯ is the case, or something.
  Ilmen (2019-01-25): See also #sıujēo and #lısīu.
  Laqme (2019-01-25): Some context: I wanted something fun and slangy-sounding and easy to say, and more {ie'o}-like and {sa'enai}-like than literally “similar to / approximate to having such-and-such-property”.  You can throw it anywhere as an adverbial interjection («rãrā») and it also works well in serials («rárā chủe»). This is apriori-ish, but duplicating the word for “or” seemed fitting, because that’s what a few languages I speak do, at least.
  uakci (2019-01-25): +1
  Hoemai (2019-01-30): +1
  fagri (2019-12-19): +1
  Dembim (2026-02-10): Pronominal_class: hoq
  Dembim (2026-02-10): Frame: 0

  el (2022-07-13): ^ or in the form «X rı Y rı Z rı ...»
  el (2022-07-13): difference between this and official **rı**: this isn't a connective question, it's explicitly a choice
  el (2022-07-13): if none of the answers apply: **nazy**
  el (2022-07-26): responses don't necessarily have to be Nko, but it's still a "choose one" question
  uakci (2022-07-26): +1 for the overall idea, but i don't think there needs to be an implicit requirement that the answer be one-pronged – there are situations where it makes sense to have that and situations where it doesn't, and it's better to leave that to context

⚠ rıoq [(c c) (d n) hóq F] @oldofficial 2019-05-05 #Sv2rMAqthm $en -1 %basic
predicate: ‘type’; ▯ is a type/kind of ▯
  fagri (2019-11-30): Should this be used with two (1) types? As with "Rỉoq lî kảtō na lî nỉaı da"? In this version, it would essentially be saying that the first property is a "subset" of the second one.
  fagri (2019-11-30): The Toaq version would be: "Mẻa lú jẻo hóa ráı[1] na lú jẻo hóa ráı[2]"
  uakci (2022-06-05): this word is perhaps best understood as a kind predicate (now that we have kinds, it’s only natural!), so e.g., **rỉoq baq kủochaı baq chảı** for ‘black tea is a kind of tea’. for properties, you may use **lıca**. for concretes, you may use **mea**
  uakci (2022-11-01): perhaps more interestingly we could regard this predicate as being an implication operator over kinds: ∀X. lo(x₁)(X) → lo(x₂)(X). see **lo**

rıꝡeq @uakci 2024-02-15 #EXB23QKLR $en +1
▯ is a ▯ of type ▯; ▯ is an instance of kind ▯, specifically the ▯ subkind of it.
  uakci (2024-02-15): think of this word as a gluing-together of **eq** and **rıoq**: the first two slots are coupled with **eq** and the last two are coupled with **rıoq**
  uakci (2024-02-15): **rıꝡeq ní báq rua hí?** ‘what type of flower is this?’ is to be understood as **eq ní hí rıoq bâq rua?**
  uakci (2024-02-15): if the indirect object is omitted, we get the more elliptical ‘what type [of flower] is this flower?’: **rıꝡeq ní rua hí?**, which is equivalent to **eq ní rua hí rıoq?**

se- @uakci 2023-10-25 #skgFif30d $en +1
prefix: turn verb X into verb: ▯ is in a state resulting from having X’d.
  uakci (2023-10-25): this is slightly different from **luı** because it emphasizes the new state, this state being the end result of the action, rather than the action itself. it implies that some change was made and the subject is persisting that change
  uakci (2023-10-25): you could also perhaps argue that a closed door *is* **luı rıasho** or **tẹrıatua** although that is petty and not up-to-speed with pragmatics and **mala** exists for that semantic space already anyway
  uakci (2023-10-25): so perhaps this is reminiscent of the german zustandspassiv (‘is opened’ as opposed to ‘has opened’)

sema [(c) (d) tá F] @Laqme 2025-03-20 #_uCXd3G89 $en +3
▯ is/are an "intensional plural entity" (Int Pl e).
  Laqme (2025-03-20): A **sema** is, under the hood, a function from worlds to plural constants. In kuna, this is the most basic denotation of an **aqmı**. For example, **túq gochıq** more or less denotes a **sema** that selects all cats from the world.
  Laqme (2025-03-20): This concept is worthy of some primacy because the "environment of bound pronouns" can be thought of as a mapping from pronouns to **sema**-s.
  Laqme (2025-03-20): Etymology: from their effective type signature "s → {e}" and the nominalizing suffix **ma**.

soq [(c 1i) (n d) tá F] @uakci 2022-01-09 #8FtyCn9fR $en +6 %amount,statistical_evaluation,with_greatness_property
▯ satisfies property ▯ the most.
  uakci (2022-01-09): this definition works flawlessly despite serial right-branching – e.g., **soq hủı pỏq** ‘the worst person’, **nı sỏq chủı tỉsha nỉ** ‘the first two to arrive here’
  uakci (2022-01-09): actually, »despite« isn’t even the right word here. the complement of a **soq** serial can be attributive or predicative. if it’s predicative (say, A B), then **soq** sees the property of satisfying A the most (while also being B); if attributive, we still get valid semantics for things like **nuı kảnı** ‘small rabbit’, since the **nuı**-est **kảnı** is also the **nuı**-est thing that is also a **kanı**
  uakci (2022-01-09): in other words, since the extent of an adjectival serial A B is proportional to the extent of A and not A × B (barring situations where B is flat-out false), **soq** will look at the correct »part« of the serial
  uakci (2022-01-09): yet another way to look at it: in an adjectival serial such as A B, the A part is qualitative (how *much* something is A), whereas the B part is quantitative (*if* that thing is B *at all*). so rather than A × B we have something like the *truth value* of B times the *extent* of Aa
  uakci (2022-01-09): if you agree with these postulates, you'll find this definition of **soq** unobjectionable
  uakci (2022-01-09): CORRECTION: the example beginning in **nı sỏq chủı…** should read **gu sỏq chủı…** instead
  el (2022-01-11): this definition also works very well with **meabı**, and that means **suı** can be defined similar to this

soq [(c c 1x) (d d d) tá F] @fagri 2018-09-24 #yXBeLlHeW $en +3 %amount,statistical_evaluation,with_greatness_property
(fork of official) predicate: ▯, among ▯, is the ▯est;  ▯, among ▯, satisfies property ▯ the most
  fagri (2018-09-24): This is great, because it behaves much more nicely in serial predicates.  +1 ;)
  uakci (2018-09-24): +1!
  Laqme (2019-02-12): +1 ♥
  Ilmen (2025-10-31): officialized: yes

  Ilmen (2026-02-11): discriminator: 3

  SuperSpaceNiM (2024-08-16): X-ейший, самый X-ый

sóq @uakci 2023-01-21 #1cKmcwq_t $en +1
focus particle: the most
  uakci (2023-01-21): extending hoemaı's analysis of english focus adverbs such as 'only' and 'even', which actually pair with sentence-level stress-based focus, we can see that 'the most' works similarly: '*I* like apples the most' (more than other people) vs. 'I like *apples* the most' (more than I do other fruit/food). **soq** used to be really inconvenient in convoluted situations and usually required a confusing **lu** clause to work at all, whereas this approach should feel much more natural
  uakci (2023-01-21): see also 'prior work': **sojı** (which is a RU)
  uakci (2023-01-21): related grammatical discussion (esp. the 'association with focus' part) https://toaq.net/refgram/syntax/#focusing_adverbs
  robintown (2023-11-06): this is maybe a good translation for 'especially'

shao [(c 1i) (d d) hó I] @uakci 2023-09-04 #FcGl3tIP1 $en +5 %emotion
▯ wants to satisfy property ▯.
  uakci (2023-09-04): rationale: this is easily the least intuitive thing about toaq to learners. they have to be told to use **ıqshao** for this frame. meanwhile, ‘to want to X’ is a super super super common catenative/modal verb/inflection in most languages i’m familiar with, and our departure from this seems to benefit nobody in particular. for (c 0) frame i’d suggest **faqshao** or perhaps **shao gaı**

  lassc (2025-03-20): wish, desire
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Frame: c 1i
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Pronominal_class: ho

teasaora [(c)  hó ] @Hoemai 2019-04-01 #6rGZWL3HV $en +1 %lifeform,animal,vertebrate,sauropsid
▯ is a member of the superorder Dinosauria (the true dinosaurs).
  Hoemai (2019-04-01): "Dinosaur" is from Ancient Greek δεινός (“terrible, fearful”) + σαῦρος (“lizard”), which is an alternative form of σαύρᾱ. δεινός (from PIE *dwey- (“fear”)) sounds like and is close in meaning to **tea**, while **saorā** sounds like σαύρᾱ but also happens to includes **sao**, which means "large", which is very fitting for dinosaurs. This compound is thus a lucky coincidence on two levels.
  Ilmen (2026-01-28): frame: c
  Ilmen (2026-01-28): pronominal_class: ho

teoqleo @uakci 2023-11-01 #juWGMIPio $en ±
▯ undergoes questioning/examining/interviewing with questions ▯; ▯ is tried/asked ▯.
  uakci (2023-11-01): a very broad word, roughly: ▯ is in a situation where they are meant to share information by answering questions; ▯ has questions coming at them [in some formalized way].
  uakci (2023-11-01): more specifically, could refer to: being examined / taking an exam / pop quiz / trivia quiz; being surveyed/polled / handed a questionnaire; being interviewed (as an employee; on the radio); being tried (as in witch trials), trialed (in court); questioned (by the police), put into doubt (wallslammed and forced to confess); bearing solicited witness/testimony; being rapid-fired icebreakers on a first date; so on so forth
  uakci (2023-11-01): interrogated, interrogation

  Ilmen (2018-09-26): Example: «Tủa sỉq jí níoq na ru shìu tóhōq bı tảofā nủo da.»

prefix: "sequential participation": accepts a transitive verb X which retains its valence and returns the verb:▯ does X back to ▯
  seaqtaq (2024-07-16): E.g. "Kaqla náq líq jôı, é tọıkaqla líq náq", or "The man looked and the woman, and the woman looked back at the man"

tuq @Laqme 2021-10-04 #YR5BR-BNA $ja -1
限定詞：「全ての…」（全体記号、McKayでのΛ∃）
  uakci (2021-10-04): この量化系(SA)助詞とは、補語の述語動詞句を『最多数の(複数論理的)一定値』(*maximal reference*)に変化するもの。
  uakci (2021-10-04): それからこういうmaximal referenceが求まれない可能性がある訳である。例えば「**tuq shỉ rủa**」の「**shı rủa**『一輪の花である』」という述語動詞句は、「非分配法則的」（*nondistributive*)なものなので、「一輪だけで全ての花」のことが集めれることが阻止されてしまうのである。

ꝡarı [(c c) (d d) hó A] @salarua 2025-05-20 #7vd0ieAJw $en +4
▯ defiles/desecrates/profanes/violates ▯
  salarua (2025-05-20): i.e. to damage something considered special, sacred, or protected. Need not be something physical.
  salarua (2025-05-21): Not to be used lightly. This word is not about any specific acts, but rather it's about their effects — an ẹꝡarı is purposeful, malicious, and difficult or impossible to recover from. An ẹꝡarı against an object would compromise its sanctity, purity, or other intangible quality, even if the damage is reversible (e.g. ruining someone's deeply sentimental keepsake, vandalizing a holy site with racist graffiti). An ẹꝡarı against a person would be something traumatizing (I shall not list examples).
  mimiaoganhaqpy (2025-05-21): +1: Very interesting word.  I like it.
  lassc (2025-05-21): very good
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Pronominal_class: ho
  Dembim (2026-02-04): Frame: c c

ꝡoı- @uakci 2024-05-08 #MOBDaiPdO $en ±

ꝡöı @uakci 2024-05-08 #xvhpKLLgw $en ±
faux relative clause marker piecing a noun form and a subject-truncated clause together into a full clause
  uakci (2024-05-08): this is mostly to be used as a repair strategy to turn a noun form into a predication involving that noun form in afterthought. for instance, **báq rua ꝡöı he sala râo ní cao** ≈ ‘flowers… unfolding in this season’ = **ꝡä he sala báq rua râo ní cao**
  uakci (2024-05-08): a “subject-truncated clause” here is meant in the sense of **lá**, i.e., a CP with the subject slot prefilled

zoı- @seaqtaq 2024-10-18 #Bopd4YcnA $en ±
prefix: 'antipassive': demotes greatly the saliency of an object as well as removes it from the structure of a verb. The former object may only be referenced through oblique semantic role marking
  seaqtaq (2024-10-18): . Tag: voice operator; Zọıchuq mí Joq" == "John eats [something]". Object is only referenceable obliquely through choosing the appropriate semantic role marker. "Zọıchuq mí Joq têcha háq" == "John is eating, with what's being eaten being the food" (John is even more salient than he would be in an active relation)

cheo heo @fagri 2019-12-28 #rm7cJYIh6 $en ±
▯ are all different in property ▯ (2-ary); ▯ are fully-characterized by what they ▯
  fagri (2019-12-28): Eg "durations are characterized by their size" means that the concept of "duration" has only one degree of freedom (size), which in still other words means that no two durations can have the same size (and "if they did", then they would actually be same duration)
  fagri (2019-12-28): To give another example, people are NOT characteriszed by their names: two people can have the same name yet be different people, so "chẻo hẻo mủ chủa túq pỏq" is FALSE

- dıq @uakci 2023-06-08 #nkL7th0iy $en ±

  uakci (2023-06-08): **jurıdı** ‘to be lesbian’ → **jurıdıq** ‘to be [being/acting] lesbian towards ▯’

dote po ké muq lıo @salarua 2025-03-17 #4VwXkQlET $en ±
idiom: ‘gift from the left hand’; a superficially altruistic act with a self-serving ulterior motive
  salarua (2025-03-17): The advent of modern hygiene in Toaqgua has made "clean" and "dirty" hands unnecessary, but some cultural connotations remain. It is considered uncouth at best and offensive at worst to give or accept a gift with the left hand.

pseudo-suffix: akin to suffixed «<che>» but for non-agents (tools, elements, components), forming words for objects that have passive purpose/function

  Hoemai (2023-04-07): Possible examples include **gıela** "to embrace/hug", **laıla** "to spell", **noqsaıla** "to season"

▯ is a falling diphthong mark (from the Derani writing system)
  fagri (2023-01-06): So named because «aıpu» is the word used to form «aı'aıpu», the Toaq word for the diphthong «aı»

laı bạkuqtoaı @fagri 2023-01-06 #zoCGvagye $en ±
▯ is a termination mark (the punctuation mark used to end non-declarative, non-interrogative sentences in the Derani writing system)

  fagri (2023-01-06): I *think* «lao» is an appropriate etymology but anyone should feel free to correct this with a better entry

Ꝡä tı súq ní, bï jaı jí. @robintown 2023-10-29 #sL7_nzMNF $en +1
I'm happy that you're here.
  robintown (2023-10-30): Lynn and I had a discussion about this, where we concluded that neither **kûı** or **gêm** really make sense for this meaning. As adjuncts, both constructions imply that you are, on the whole, happy. But imagine a situation in which you're having a bad day, and a friend comes to comfort you: you might say "I'm happy that you're here" even if it wouldn't be genuine to say "I'm happy". We found a paper https://www.semanticsarchive.net/Archive/DIzYWZkM/clauses.doc which claims that the clausal constituent participates in the semantics by a "regarding" relation, which suggests that a topic is the appropriate construction in Toaq. And there's similar evidence in Japanese to support this.

⚠ say @uakci 2021-08-23 #IzaBjQ3qm $en -1
quantifier: some X, if any
  uakci (2021-08-23): **P say Q** is taken to mean: ∃x.Px ⇒ [∃x.Px] Qx. in other words, the claim is tautologically true if the quantification fails
  uakci (2021-08-23): also known as existential quantification without existential import – that is to say, unlike **sa P**, **say P** doesn't presuppose that P is satisfiable (that an x such that Px exists to begin with)
